coredata.dc:title: Effects of surface modification and size on oral drug delivery of mesoporous silica formulation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ying
Cui, Yu
Zhao, Yating
Zhao, Qinfu
He, Bing
Zhang, Qiang
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: The surface chemistry and size of nanoparticles can greatly impact their interaction with biological pathways and alter efficacy. However, the interplay between surface modification and particle size has not been well investigated especially for oral delivery. It is necessary to maximize the bioavailability of loading therapeutics. Here, we prepared different sized mesoporous silica nanoparticles (100–500 nm) and conjugated them with polyethylenimine-coated carbondots (PCD) for effective transepithelial absorption. The nanoparticles were also coated with polyethylene glycol (PEG) polymers for improved mucus permeability. These mesoporous silica nanoparticles conjugated to PCD and coated in PEG (MSN@PCD@PEG) were used to study the influence of particle size and surface chemistry on transepithelial transport and bioavailability. Results demonstrated that the MSN@PCD@PEG with a diameter 250 nm had the highest transepithelial transport and oral bioavailability compared to other formulations. Drug release, endocytosis pathways, transepithelial transport and degradation of these different nanocarriers were systematically studied in order to investigate effects of size variety. The findings indicated that nanoparticle-based oral drug delivery can be potentially improved by adjusting physicochemical properties. We believe that understanding the importance of surface chemistry and particle size in the oral delivery will improve nanoparticle engineering and oral application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface chemistry
Size effect
Oral delivery
Mesoporous silica
Nanoformulation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface modification of MSNs with PCD and PEG
The stability of different nanocarriers in physiological conditions
Cellular uptake and transcellular transport study
The Initial Phases of interaction between nanocarriers and cellular membranes
Effects of surface modification on stability, cell uptake and transport pathways of nanoparticles
Effect of size on uptake, transmembrane transport and oral bioavailability of nanoparticles
The mechanisms about the difference of oral bioavailability caused by particle size effect

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (No. 2015CB932100 ); and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81473165 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of MSN@PCD@PEG. The insets are photos of this nanoparticle in aqueous solution under visible (left) and 365 nm UV (right) illumination. (B) The hydrodynamic size of nanocarriers. (C) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of nanocarriers. (D) The TGA curves of different samples.

(A) The photographs of MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG dispersed in physiological saline with a concentration of 2 mg/mL. All these photographs are taken at different times. (B) The particle size of MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG nanoparticles dispersed in distilled water and physiological saline. (C) Unadsorbed BSA amount of MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG. (D) Representative trajectories of the Brownian movement of nanoparticles in mucus. (E) CLSM images of Caco-2 cells after incubation with different nanoparticles. Red indicated cell membranes, green indicates MSN nanoparticles, and blue indicates MSN@PCD@PEG nanoparticles. (F) Intracellular internalization of different nanoparticles on Caco-2 cells after incubation with endocytosis inhibitors. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The CLSM images of rat intestine 2 h after oral administration of different nanoparticles. Red indicated cell membranes, green indicates MSN nanoparticles, and blue indicates MSN@PCD@PEG nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) TEM images and hydrodynamic size of different sized MSN@PCD@PEG. (B) CLSM images of the distribution of different nanoparticles on Caco-2 cell monolayer from apical to basolateral side. Blue, nanoparticles; red, cell membrane; green, transwell membrane. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The CLSM images of rat intestine 4 h after oral administration of different nanoformulations. Blue, nanoparticles; red, cell membrane; green, coumarin-6. (B) Variation of serum fenofibrate level after orally administering loaded nanoparticles and commercial capsule at dose of 30 mg/kg. Data are means ± SD (n = 5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Release profiles of fenofibrate from different formulations. (B) The QCM analysis of the interaction between nanoparticles with Caco-2 cells. (C) The cell uptake of nanoparticles after incubation with endocytosis inhibitors.

(A) CLSM and TEM images of Caco-2 cells after incubation with different samples. Blue: nanoparticles; red: cell membrane. (B) The degradation of different nanoparticles after incubation with simulated intestinal fluid. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of surface modification and size on oral drug delivery of mesoporous silica formulation.

head.ce:title: Effects of surface modification and size on oral drug delivery of mesoporous silica formulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Peking University, Beijing 100191, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmaceutics, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The surface chemistry and size of nanoparticles can greatly impact their interaction with biological pathways and alter efficacy. However, the interplay between surface modification and particle size has not been well investigated especially for oral delivery. It is necessary to maximize the bioavailability of loading therapeutics. Here, we prepared different sized mesoporous silica nanoparticles (100–500 nm) and conjugated them with polyethylenimine-coated carbondots (PCD) for effective transepithelial absorption. The nanoparticles were also coated with polyethylene glycol (PEG) polymers for improved mucus permeability. These mesoporous silica nanoparticles conjugated to PCD and coated in PEG (MSN@PCD@PEG) were used to study the influence of particle size and surface chemistry on transepithelial transport and bioavailability. Results demonstrated that the MSN@PCD@PEG with a diameter 250 nm had the highest transepithelial transport and oral bioavailability compared to other formulations. Drug release, endocytosis pathways, transepithelial transport and degradation of these different nanocarriers were systematically studied in order to investigate effects of size variety. The findings indicated that nanoparticle-based oral drug delivery can be potentially improved by adjusting physicochemical properties. We believe that understanding the importance of surface chemistry and particle size in the oral delivery will improve nanoparticle engineering and oral application.

ce:section.ce:para: Oral delivery is a preferred administration pathway for most therapeutic cargos since it has the best patient compliance and fewer safety issues compared to intravenous delivery [1–3]. However, challenges for oral delivery still exist especially for drugs with weak stability and low solubility in the GI tract and poor permeability across epithelial tissue. For many years the goal has been to enhance the oral bioavailability of most therapeutics via simple drug delivery systems. To achieve the goal, nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems are attracting more attention since they can protect drugs from premature degradation and interaction with the physiological environment, enhance drug absorption into a special tissues, and increase intracellular penetration [4–6]. Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs), different from traditional organic polymers and lipid-based nanoparticles, possess high surface area, adjustable pore size, good biocompatibility and simple surface modification. For these reasons, MSNs have become a promising vehicle to enhance oral bioavailability of hydrophobic therapeutics [7–9]. Zhang et al. developed spherical MSNs through an organic template method and demonstrated that MSNs could effectively enhance loading efficiency of poorly water-soluble drug and improve drug dissolution rate and permeability [10,11]. Popat et al. further designed bioresponsive MSNs which were sensitive to pH and enzymes. The MSNs had programmable drug release in GI tract and high drug loading of sulfasalazine [12]. Several studies have shown the application of multifunctional modified MSNs in tumor targeted therapy and the promise of MSNs as drug delivery systems with adjustable surface chemistry and controlled drug release [13–15].

Since previous studies have reported that a slight change in physicochemistry of nanoparticles may significantly impact their interaction with biological pathways and alter efficacy, it is necessary to find new ways to maximize the bioavailability of loading therapeutics [16,17]. Lu et al. studied the effect of size on cancer cell uptake of MSNs (30–280 nm) and found that the maximum uptake occurred with 50 nm MSNs [18]. The effect of the shape of MSNs on the endocytosis, biodistribution, excretion and biocompatibility was separately studied by Li et al. and Shao et al. [19,20]. Most of these studies with MSNs were focused on intravenous administration and not on oral delivery. Without detailed studies on the impact of MSN physicochemistry on the GI tract and bioavailability, the application of MSNs towards oral delivery is limited.

In this study, we constructed a MSNs library composed of surface modification (bilayer polymer conjugated) and different size (100–500 nm) to explore the role of surface chemistry and particle size in oral drug delivery. As is reported, nanoparticles have to overcome the mucus barriers and penetrate the epithelial cells for efficient oral delivery. Hydrophilic/neutral nanoparticles are necessary for mucus permeation, but hydrophobic/cationic nanoparticles are important for efficient epithelial absorption. Therefore, we developed a bilayer modification on the surface of MSNs (d = 480 nm) consisting of polyethylenimine coated-carbondots (PCD) for effective transepithelial absorption and polyethylene glycol (PEG) polymers for improved mucus permeability (called as MSN@PCD@PEG, @ means modified by). In addition, PCD can also be utilized to real-time monitor the nanoparticle distribution and trafficking in vivo. Bilayer modification showed that the surface chemistry of MSNs does affect biological interactions and bioavailability. The modification enhanced stability in the physiological environment, altered cell uptake mechanisms and increased distribution in various intestinal sections. However, this improvement in oral bioavailability was limited so we further focused on the size factor to determine whether the optimum effect could be obtained by adjusting particle size. MSN@PCD@PEG with different diameters (100 nm, 250 nm and 480 nm) were developed. The most significant enhancement in transepithelial transport, intestinal distribution and oral bioavailability was observed for MSN250@PCD@PEG. MSN100@PCD@PEG came in second while MSN480@PCD@PEG, the formulation used to compare with unmodified MSNs, exhibited the least improvement. Drug release profiles, interactions with the cell membrane, endocytosis pathways, transepithelial transport and degradation of these engineered MSNs in gastric intestinal juice were systematically studied in order to investigate effects of size variety.

In summary, we confirmed the importance of surface chemistry and particle size in oral delivery applications of MSNs and established the proof of concept for further studies in optimizing oral bioavailability of nanoparticles by adjusting their physicochemical properties.

Cetyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), pluronicF127, citrate and ethylenediamine (EA) were obtained from Shan Dong Yu Wang Reagent Company (China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), polyethylenimine (PEI, MW:2000), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDCI), polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW:5000), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and fenofibrate (FBB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents utilized in study were analytic grade.

Caco-2 cell line was purchased from National Platform of Experimental Cell Resources for Sci-Tech (Beijing, China). Cells were cultured in minimum essential medium (MEM), containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum, 1% (v/v) nonessential amino-acid, 1% (v/v) pyruvic acid sodium salt and 1% penicillin and streptomycin (100 IU/mL) at 37 °C with 5% CO2.

SD rats (female, 6–8 weeks) were from Vital River Laboratory Animal Center (Beijing, China). All animal studies were performed with the approval of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Peking University.

Different sized MSNs were prepared by adjusting the amount of F127 according to the previous reports [21]. Briefly, 0.5 g CTAB and triblock copolymer F127 (0 g, 1 g or 2 g) were dissolved in 98 mL distilled water, in which 12 mL 29 wt% ammonium hydroxide solution and 40 mL ethanol were added. Then, 1.9 mL TEOS was introduced to the mixture under intensive stirring, and the reaction was maintained at room temperature for 24 h. After that, an ATS AH110D homogenizer (ATS Engineer Inc., Shanghai, China) was performed to homogenize the resulting precipitate. The synthesized nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation and re-dispersed in 300 mL absolute ethanol containing 1 g NH4NO3 to reflux at 80 °C overnight for template extraction. The final particles were centrifuged, washed with ethanol and distilled water for 3 times and dried overnight at room temperature in vacuum. Here, 0 g, 1 g and 2 g of F127 were used respectively to obtain the different sized MSNs (called as MSN480, MSN250 and MSN100).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of MSNs with PCD and PEG
ce:section.ce:para: The PCD was prepared by the hydrothermal method described in our earlier report [22]. Polyethylenimine (PEI), citric acid and ethylenediamine were added into a beaker, then the reaction mixture was heated to 160 °C and kept for 6 h. The final reaction products were dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off: 3500) against water for 24 h to remove small molecules. Then PCD in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain the brown solid. To perform PCD coating, 10 mg of synthesized MSNs was suspended in 1 mL of 5 mg/mL PCD solution and stirred for 1 h. Then the resulting MSN@PCD was centrifuged and washed to remove excess PCD. To perform PEG attachment, methoxypoly ethylene glycol (m-PEG) was used due to the single reactive hydroxyl group for PEI coating. In order to activate m-PEG, the hydroxyl group was replaced with NHSester which is able to react with PEI amine residues. Typically, 10 mg of MSN@PCD was suspended in 1.5 mL of DMF and then 50 mg of activated m-PEG was added to above solutions under stirring for 24 h. The nanoparticles were centrifuged and washed with DMF and ethanol to obtain MSN@PCD@PEG.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai G2 20 instrument, FEI, USA) was used to investigate the morphology and size of different mesoporous silica nanoparticles. To test the successful modification and fluorescent capability of carbon dots in MSNs, fluorescence spectra was recorded on a multifunctional microplate reader (SpectraMax M5, Molecular Devices, USA). Different layers modification was confirmed by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (SA3100 surface area analyzer, Beckman Coulter, USA), zeta potential change (Malvern Zetasize NanoZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) and Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometry (FT-IR) spectra recorded on an FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker IFS 55, Switzerland) by compressing the samples into pellets with KBr. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by a TGA-50 instrument (Shimadzu, Japan) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen purge of 40 mL/min.

Drug loading efficiency was determined by treating different formulations with methanol solution under ultrasonication, then the extraction was analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometry ((UV-2000, UNICO, U.S.A.). Drug loading (%) = (the weight of drug in samples/the weight of samples) × 100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of different nanocarriers in physiological conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrodynamic diameter and photographs of different MSNs dispersed in physiological conditions and distilled water were determined to test their stability. In order to mimic the situation of our formulations in the physiological conditions, pure MSNs and modified MSNs were incubated in the physiological saline and distilled water for 24 h at 37 °C and shaken at 120 rpm. Then the changes of hydrodynamic diameter were recorded.

The behavior of different formulations within the mucus layer was measured by incubating particles in mucin [23,24]. MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG were mixed with the mucin solutions at a concentration of 1 mg/ml, respectively. The motion of different nanoparticles in mucin solutions was observed using a real-time multiple-particle tracking method [25,26]. Their motion trajectories over 6 s were recoded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and transcellular transport study
ce:section.ce:para: Caco-2 cells were seeded in 24-well plates for quantitation of uptake and into confocal dishes for confocal microscopy at a density of 2 × 105 cells/ml. Flow cytometry (FCM) was used to estimate the cellular uptake of nanoparticles. Cells were treated with various nanoparticles for 3 h, rinsed with cold PBS, detached by trypsin and suspended in 300 μL of PBS. The fluorescence intensity of nanoparticles was measured (Ex: 405 nm; Em: 470 nm or Ex: 488 nm; Em: 504 nm) by FCM with 10,000 events collected. For the visualization of uptake of MSN@PCD@PEG on Caco-2 cells, the cell nucleus was stained with Hoechst 33258 (blue) for Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) assay using a Leica DM-6000 CS microscope (Leica Instruments Inc., Wetzlar, Germany) or the cells were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for cell TEM. CLSM using Z-axial scanning was utilized to observe the transcellular transport efficiency of different sized nanoparticles. To investigate the cell uptake mechanism of nanoparticles, the different specific endocytotic inhibitors were used. Caco-2 cells were incubated with inhibitors for 30 min, and then treated with nanoparticles for another 2.5 h. The relative amount of uptake was measured by FCM. The mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) was measured by FACS (FACS Calibur, Becton Dickinson, U.S.A.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Initial Phases of interaction between nanocarriers and cellular membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between nanoparticles and cellular membranes was investigated by the quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) [27]. The QCM gold electrodes were immobilized with Caco-2 cells to exhibit the interaction of nanoparticles with cell membrane practically. Firstly, the gold electrodes were modified with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA) and l-lysine hydrobromide for Caco-2 cell immobilization. Subsequently, the nanoparticles were injected into the chamber after obtaining the stable baselines. The binding process was monitored by using a KSV QCM-Z500 instrument with a flow rate of 200 μL/min.

Caco-2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 2.0 × 104 and incubated in cell medium for 24 h. The medium was then removed and replaced with various samples at different concentrations. After incubation, 20 µL MTT solutions (5 mg/mL) were added to the culture medium and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. After removing the culture medium containing MTT, 150 µL DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals formed. Finally, the absorbance of the formazan was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader.

The BSA protein adsorption was measured according to the reported method. Briefly, 10 mg MSN nanoparticle samples were dispersed into 5 mL PBS solution, followed by the addition of 5 mL BSA solution. The mixture was shaken at a rate of 150 rpm at 37 °C for 4 h. The mixture was then centrifuged and the supernatant was determined by the staining method with Coomassie brilliant blue at a UV–vis absorption wavelength of 595 nm to measure the residue BSA.

Before coating, 1 ml methanol solution of FFB (100 mg/ml) was dropped to 100 mg MSNs. The mixture was gently stirred and the methanol solvent was subsequently evaporated at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was dried for 24 h. Then PCD and PEG were modified on the surface of MSNs using the method in the Section 2.3. The release of FFB from MSN@PCD@PEG were performed in the 1% SDS media. The prepared FFB-loaded MSN nanoparticle samples were suspended in the release medium and shaken at 120 rpm (37 °C). At specified times, 3 mL samples of release medium were withdrawn and replaced with an equal volume of fresh medium. The release of FFB was determined at 290 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometry ((UV-2000, UNICO, U.S.A.).

The absorption of different nanoparticles at the intestinal tract was investigated as previously described [28]. Briefly, rats were separated into two groups (n = 3), and administrated nanoparticles orally at a dose of 40 mg/kg. After 2 h, the rats were sacrificed, and duodenum, jejunum, and ileum samples were collected and prepared as frozen slices for CLSM assay to observe the intestinal distribution of nanoparticles. The unmodified MSNs were labeled by FITC. The cell membrane was stained with rhodamine phalloidin (red) for CLSM assay.

The commercial FFB capsule, different sized FFB loaded-MSN@PCD@PEG were administrated orally. Blood samples were collected at different time intervals after the administrationof 30 mg/kg, respectively. Plasma samples (100 μl were mixed with 10 μl internal standard (Diazepam, 100 μg/ml) and 1 M HCl (100 μl). Then the mixture was vortex-mixed for 1 min. Subsequently 2 ml diethylether was added to the mixture and vortex-mixed for another 5 min. After centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min, the organic phase was evaporated to dryness under the nitrogen flow. Blood FFB level was quantified using HPLC (Hitachi™, Japan). The pharmacokinetic parameters were determined using the PC software of the Drug and Statistics (DAS version 2.1.1) computer program.

The simulated intestinal fluid (pH = 6.8) was prepared according to the previous report [29]. The nanoparticles were immersed into different media with the concentration of 2.0 mg/ml at 37 °C under shaking. After 7 days, the suspensions were centrifuged and washed with 10% SDS and then the nanoparticles were characterized by TEM.

All data are represented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The graphs were plotted using Graphpad, Prism 6.0, (GraphPad Software, LaJolla, CA). Statistical analyses were conducted using t-Test and a value of p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

We have prepared modified/unmodified MSNs to investigate the role of surface modification in oral delivery. Some reporters have demonstrated that hydrophobic/cationic surface is important for efficient epithelial absorption, while hydrophilic/neutral surface of nanoparticles is necessary for mucus permeation. So here we performed the bilayer coating on the surface of MSNs via the PEI-coated carbon dots (PCD) for enhanced epithelial absorption and PEG polymer for improved mucus permeability. The PCD was first prepared by the hydrothermal method, and consisted of two units: a PEI layer for enhanced epithelial absorption due to the polycation surface, and a quantum dot with high quantum yields for real-time bioimaging. The TEM images of PCD as shown in Fig. S1A exhibited a uniform diameter of 2.8 nm and the observed lattice in the fast fourier transform (FFT) image (inserted in Fig. S1A) also suggested that prepared PCD is crystalline structure. TheX-raydiffraction(XRD) pattern of PCD displayed a broad amorphous carbon structure of graphite lattice spacing at21.5° (Fig. S1 B)[30]. In addition, PCD exhibited optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 nm and 460 nm and had a strong blue color under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S1 C, D), indicating the real-time bioimaging ability of PCD. Besides, the photoluminescence of PCD was stable and its efficiency would not be interfered by different media (Fig. S2).

Fig. 1 A showed the TEM of bilayer modified MSNs (MSN@PCD@PEG), which had a spherical morphology with diameter around 480 nm. PCD layer functioned well as a bioimaging agent since MSN@PCD@PEG can also be emitted under the 365 nm UV lamp (inserted in Fig. 1A, Fig. S4). Besides, the modification by PCD and PEG would not significantly influence the particle size and MSNs with or without these layers exhibited similar size distribution determined by DLS (Fig. 1B). Fig. 1 C showed that the adsorption-desorption isotherm of MSNs was 800 m2g−1, while their surface area was decreased sharply after further modification, confirming that PCD and PEG were successfully attached to the surface of MSNs. The zeta potential of MSN@PCD showed a highly positive charge (26.1 ± 0.862 mV), but the nanoparticles exhibited a weakly positive charge of 5.44 mV after the attachment of PEG. The biolayer grafting amount was measured by the TGA. As shown in Fig. 1D, the grafting amounts of PCD and PEG were 20% and 5%, respectively, which further confirmed the successful grafting on the surface of nanopartilces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of surface modification on stability, cell uptake and transport pathways of nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Bilayer modification of MSNs was designed majorly to enhance its mucus permeation through hydrophilic PEG layer and transepithelial activity through cationic PCD layer. However, this strategy was also found to be able to improve the stability of MSNs in physiological environment. The hydrodynamic diameter of MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG was measured in both distilled water and physiological saline, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, although MSNs were stable in distilled water, they flocculated rapidly in physiological saline due to the destruction of the original charge equilibrium in salt solutions. However, their stability under physiological environment was effectively improved after bilayer conjugation, which could be attributed to the steric hindrance between the nanoparticles and electrostatic interaction between the PCD and PEG [31–34]. Unabsorbed BSA amount for both MSNs and MSN@PCD@PEG exceeded 80% (Fig. 2C), indicating a good biocompatibility for MSNs no matter they were modified or not.

As is reported, the mucus clearance is a primary innate defense mechanism of intestinal tract to rapidly remove the cationic or hydrophobic particles. Therefore, the surface of MSN@PCD was modified with neutral hydrophilic PEG to conceal the strong polycationic property of the PCD, expected to improve the mucus permeation ability of nanocarriers. Here, mucin as the principal components of mucus was used to study the behavior of these nanoparticles within the mucus layer. The motion of different nanoparticles in mucin solutions was observed using a real-time multiple-particle tracking method [25,26]. Their motion trajectories over 6 s were shown in Fig. 2D. After the bilayer modification, MSN@PCD@PEG exhibited much improved motility in mucin solution compared with unmodified MSN, indicating that the surface modification can enhance the mucus permeability. The Caco-2 cell line was used to evaluate the epithelial uptake of nanocarriers. As shown in Fig. 2E, MSN without any modification exhibited little cell uptake and absorption, while after modification with PCD and PEG layers, its absorption and uptake were improved.

In addition, series of endocytosis inhibitors were used to identify the potential cellular uptake pathways of MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG in Caco-2 cells. As shown in Fig. 2F, the cellular uptake of MSN was significantly reduced after the addition of EIPA and cytochalasin D (CytD), suggesting an important role of macropinocytosis and actin filaments in its uptake [17,18]. Treating with CPZ, sucrose and genistein otherwise would not influence the uptake amount of MSN, which demonstrated clathrin- and caveolae-mediated endocytosis did not participate in the process [35,36]. However, modified MSN was obviously influenced by these endocytosis inhibitors, indicating that after bilayer modification, MSNs could be endocytosed via more multiple pathways, including not only clathrin and lipid raft mediated mechanism but also actin filaments and micropinocytosis anticipation. Further study on the adhesion and cellular uptake in intestinal epithelium of MSN and MSN@PCD@PEG was shown in Fig. 3 . The results illustrated that modified MSNs (blue signals) had a higher adhesion especially in the villi, demonstrating the obstacles from the mucus and epithelial cells could be overcome through addition of PCD and PEG on the surface of MSN nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of size on uptake, transmembrane transport and oral bioavailability of nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The study of dispersity and stability of nanoparticles demonstrated that the unmodified MSNs can flocculate into larger particles in the physiological environment, which may influence the studies on the size effects of MSNs. But the modification significantly improves this phenomenon of nanoparticles compared to unmodified group. Therefore, here we prepared different sized MSN@PCD@PEG nanoparticles to study the role of particle size in oral drug delivery. They are represented in the TEM with respect to their diameters (100–500 nm), called as MSN100@PCD@PEG, MSN250@PCD@PEG and MSN480@PCD@PEG. The DLS showed that the hydrodynamic diameters of these particles are ∼615 nm, 295 nm and 190 nm, respectively (Fig. 4 A and Table S1). The adsorption-desorption isotherm of different sized MSNs exhibited a similar pore size and high surface area in Fig. S5. Nanoparticles with different size can have dissimilar behaviors in vitro and in vivo and can interact with cells differently. As an effective drug delivery carrier, biocompatibility studies are essential before being used in vivo. Here we first investigated the influences of diffident sized nanoparticles on the BSA protein adsorption and cell viability. As shown in Figs. S6 and S7, there’s no significant difference among these samples. The unabsorbed BSA amount and cell viability rates exceeded 80% after incubation with different samples for 48 h at the concentrations of 50–500 µg/ml, confirming that this nanoparticle with good biocompatibility were suitable for further studies.

Then, we investigated the effect of particle size in their transport across intestinal cells. The Caco-2 cell monolayers on Transwell membrane were used to evaluated the effect of nanoparticle size on their transport across intestinal cells. The behaviors of nanoparticles on cell monolayer were observed by Z-axial scanning of CLSM. As shown in Fig. 4B, no significant transmembrane transport of differently sized particles was observed across Caco-2 monolayers. Our group previous study has shown that the PLGA nanoparticles were internalized into cells but could not transport across Caco-2 monolayer [37]. This finding is consistent with Hu’s group, who observed that high uptake of solid lipid nanoparticles but lower transcytosis after the incubation with Caco-2 cell monolayer [38]. In addition, we can also see the obvious adsorption of these nanoparticles in the cell membrane from the Caco-2 monolayer. Fenofibrate as a BCS II class drug was used to investigate the drug transepithelial permeability after being loaded in MSNs with different size. As shown in Fig. S8, the smaller sized nanoparticles exhibited a higher FFB permeability than that of large nanoparticles (size in 480 nm). In order to further investigate the size effects of nanoparticles on drug transepithelial behaviors, the transport of nanoparticles across the intestinal epithelium was evaluated and the coumarin-6 as the model drug was loaded into the nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 5 A, nanoparticles in smaller size exhibited more efficient absorption of coumarin-6. In MSN250@PCD@PEG group, most fluorescence signals of nanoparticles were detected in both the villi and epithelial cells, while very little signal were observed for MSN480@PCD@PEG. This result suggested that coumarin-6 can be efficiently absorbed at the epithelium when being loaded into smaller sized nanoparticles.

More importantly, the in vivo studies are required to verify the in vitro results. Here, we further investigated the role of different sized nanoparticles in oral bioavailability of fenofibrate- loaded nanoparticles compared with that of the commercial capsule. The pharmacokinetic profiles of fenofibrate following a single dose of each formulation were shown in Fig. 5B and the pharmacokinetic parameters were listed in Table S2. It is worth noting that the smaller sized formulations exhibited a significantly increased AUC0–∞ and Cmax. Orally administrated MSN250@PCD@PEG formulations had a highest fenofibrate plasma concentration in rats, which reached the maximum concentration at 4 h. The AUC0–∞ of fenofibrate in rats treated with MSN250@PCD@PEG was significantly enhanced over 2.7-fold than that of commercial capsule (p < 0.001), indicating that the oral bioavailability of FFB could be dramatically increased by this nanoparticle. However, the mean value of Cmax for the MSN480@PCD@PEG was not significantly increased compared with the commercial capsule (p > 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms about the difference of oral bioavailability caused by particle size effect
ce:section.ce:para: We supposed that effective drug penetration into intestinal epithelial membranes required at least two important features: completeness of drug nanocarriers and strong uptake of epithelial cells. In order to investigate the effects of particle size, drug release profile, interaction with cell membranes, endocytosis pathways, transepithelial transport and degradation of these engineered MSNs with different size were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the release profile of loading drug, fenofibrate, accelerated as the particle size of MSN@PCD@PEG decreased, indicating that nanoparticles with larger size could decrease drug release and maintain more of them loaded in the carriers. Such effect could be partly explained by that smaller particles were reported to possess larger surface area and promoted drug leaking. To compare the difference of cellular uptake of MSNs with various size, the interaction with cell membranes were first studied since stronger interaction generally hinted more adhesion and uptake into epithelial cells. As shown in Fig. 6B, there was a positive correlation between nanoparticle size and interaction with cell membranes, indicating that MSN480@PCD@PEG was expected to have the highest cell uptake. However, size difference also influenced the endocytosis pathways for MSNs (Fig. 6C), which might also affect the final uptake efficacy. For larger particles, micropinocytosis was commonly considered to play a major role in their uptake, and we also observed the similar phenomenon for MSN480@PCD@PEG since the fluorescent signal in cells decreased after addition of inhibitor EIPA. In contrast, smaller particles such as MSN250@PCD@PEG and MSN100@PCD@PEG demonstrated different mechanisms. β-CD, dynasor and sucrose inhibited their uptake significantly, indicating the participation of clathrin- and lipid raft mediated pathways.

At last, confocal and TEM images about cell uptake of different MSNs were detected to visualize the cellular uptake procedure (Fig. 7 A). MSN250@PCD@PEG exhibited the most adhesion and cell uptake, and MSN100@PCD@PEG came to the second while the uptake of MSN480@PCD@PEG was hard to observe even if it showed strongest interaction with cell membranes. The quantitative data was analyzed by flow cytometry (Fig. S9) and confirmed the uptake difference observed in visualized images. We here provided another possible explanation by investigating the degradation of different MSNs under intestinal environment (Fig. 7B). MSN480@PCD@PEG showed little difference throughout the whole incubation time, while the surface of MSN250@PCD@PEG became a little roughness. This phenomenon was more obvious in MSNs with size around 100 nm, meaning that smaller particle would be easier to be degraded. Combining the high surface area and degradation feature, MSN100@PCD@PEG showed the fastest drug leaking, which could lead to precipitation of free drug in intestine (data not shown), further limiting its oral bioavailability.

Based on all these results, size effect was demonstrated to play an important role in the bioavailability of MSNs through influencing at least two important features-drug leaking and cellular uptake. Particles with large size around 500 nm showed good drug maintaining ability because of less surface area and slow degradation in gastric intestinal environment. Larger MSNs (MSN480@PCD@PEG) also exhibited strong interaction with cell membranes and high cell attachment whereas its cell uptake was significantly limited, possibly caused by low efficient micropinocytosis for large particles. In contrast, MSNs with small size around 100 nm had good cell attachment and uptake even though the effect was somewhat compromised due to weak interaction with cell membranes. However, MSN100@PCD@PEG exhibited the fastest degradation and most drug leaking in the GI tract, which caused the decrease of the bioavailability. MSNs with size between 500 nm and 100 nm was demonstrated to possess maximum benefits because of its balance in drug leaking, interaction with cell membranes and endocytosis efficiency. These interesting results offered a useful instruction to design silica nanoparticles with appropriate size to achieve optimal oral bioavailability.

Mesoporous silica nanoparticle-based delivery systems have been extensively used in improving the oral bioavailability of loaded therapeutics. In this study, for the first time, the surface modification and size effects of MSNs on oral delivery were investigated both in vitro and vivo. As shown in Fig. 8 A, MSNs without modification easily aggregated [39] and stuck to the mucus layer, leading to poor bioavailability. Bilayer modification with synthesized PEI-coated carbon dots (PCD) and PEG layers rendered the particles more stable in the GI environment and helped overcome the mucus diffusion and epithelium absorption barriers, which indicated that the surface modification can improve the oral bioavailability. Some reports have said that the shape of nanoparticles plays an important role in oral drug delivery [19,40]. Here, modified MSNs with different diameters also exhibited distinct oral efficacies. MSN480@PCD@PEG could protect drug from leaking but showed limited cellular uptake (Fig. 8B). MSN100@PCD@PEG adhered to the GI tract and was taken up easily but would experience faster degradation, with most drug releasing and precipitating in the GI tract (Fig. 8D). MSN250@PCD@PEG exhibited both efficient cellular uptake and low drug leaking percentage, making bilayer modified MSNs with size between 100 nm and 500 nm most promising drug carriers in oral delivery systems (Fig. 8C). This optimal size was suggested for the composite material to achieve the highest oral bioavailability.
This work was supported by National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (No. 2015CB932100); and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81473165).
The authors report no conflicts of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.11.065.
